data.title: Instacart: Macro Weakness Likely To Bite In Coming Quarters
data.description:
Instacart's (NASDAQ:CART) business is steadily progressing at the moment, despite its share price coming under pressure in recent months. I previously suggested that rising customer acquisition costs were a concern. This is likely being driven by increased competition, which may limit Instacart's ability to improve its margins longer term. On a positive note, Instacart's financials continue to improve as the company moves past post-pandemic headwinds.

While Instacart has a solid business, I believe that convergence between categories will become a more important theme over the next decade, significantly changing competitive dynamics. In the near-term, macro weakness is likely to pressure consumer spending and I believe that this will dampen demand for Instacart's services.

Market Conditions

Fulfillment costs are crucial to the profitability of grocery delivery and are driven by pick-and-pack and delivery. An analysis by McKinsey suggests online delivery costs amount to around 17% of a $100 order, which is high relative to the typical single digit margins for grocers. This is an interesting situation, as the majority of online grocery shoppers now prefer home delivery to click and collect. There is also less willingness to bear these costs in the form of fees and higher pricing as delivery becomes viewed as the norm.

Instacart's delivery fees, tips, and markups add roughly 25% to the cost of an order, which is likely a key reason that ecommerce hasn't achieved greater penetration of the grocery vertical. Instacart is pushing for grocers to offer equal pricing online though and is lowering the bar for free delivery. For example, Instacart recently lowered its minimum basket size for free delivery for Instacart+ members from $35 to $10.

While I generally expect this situation to persist, due to the potential for low-cost delivery to drive sales, automation is the only way for this to be done sustainably. Automation is a positive for the grocery delivery category as a whole, but it threatens to undermine Instacart's competitive positioning. For example, Walmart (WMT) is investing in microfulfillment centers and likely wants to control online ordering and delivery.

The more immediate concern for Instacart is its sensitivity to the business cycle and potential weakness in consumer spending. I believe that delivery is a luxury that will be cut in the event of macro weakness. CPG ad spend is also something that is likely to come under pressure in a recession. While a downturn is still hypothetical at this point, tariffs, combined with the precarious state of the labor and housing markets, create a setup with elevated downside risk. Instacart has never navigated a genuine recession, making it difficult to predict what will happen, but I would not be surprised to see a 0-10% drop in revenue in the event of even a small recession.

Instacart Business Updates

Instacart's core grocery business has a dominant position in the market at the moment, as illustrated by its more than 70% share of baskets worth over $75 and the fact it continues to add more new users than competitors. Instacart is also better than its peers at converting small basket customers to large basket customers, making the company an attractive partner for grocers. Within this part of the business, Instacart is focused on growing users and order frequency, in part through a focus on affordability. This includes new service options like Super Saver and Free Pickup and EBT SNAP acceptance.

Instacart is also trying to integrate its business with grocers, potentially creating a sizeable barrier to entry. This includes both things like loyalty program integrations and Instacart's enterprise solutions. The value proposition for grocers is essentially that Instacart's solutions can help accelerate growth. Instacart now has 1,800 retail banners on its marketplace and powers 600 enterprise storefronts. The company also launched 30 net new retailer sites in 2024, more than double the prior year.

There remains a large opportunity internationally, which Instacart now appears to be pursuing more aggressively. For example, Instacart has started to deploy Caper Carts with Coles in Australia. Caper Carts are shopping carts that feature a screen, barcode scanner and electronic payment capabilities.

Advertising is a focus area for Instacart, although growth of this part of the business is only modest. Instacart recently introduced AI-enabled automation tools to help advertisers manage campaigns and optimize ad performance. These tools are currently in pilot with select brands. While improved ad performance and lower barriers to adoption are positives, increased use of AI and automation plays to the strengths of organizations with greater scale. As a result, it is not surprising to see Instacart's advertising growth trailing other retail media networks.

There are now over 7,000 active brands on Instacart's platform, with spend exceeding a $1 billion annual run rate in Q4. Emerging brands are an area of particular strength at the moment. Instacart also has more than 220 Carrot Ads partners, a program where Instacart powers ads on partner properties. These partners contribute inventory and help improve performance.

Financial Analysis

Instacart's GTV increased 10% YoY in Q4, with orders up 11% and average order value declining 1%. The decline in average order value was attributed to restaurant orders. Lower minimum order sizes could also be a headwind to average order value going forward. Instacart believes that its affordability initiatives are a growth driver, though.

Advertising and other revenue increased 10% YoY in Q4. While this growth is solid, it isn't overly impressive given the nascent state of Instacart's advertising business and the strong growth being generated by others in the retail media space at the moment. Instacart's weakness relative to other retail media platforms could just be due to the fact that the company is over indexed to food and beverage advertising, though. Advertising revenue appears to be growing in line with the rest of the business, which may limit Instacart's ability to drive margins substantially higher. The company's long-term target is for ad revenue to total 4-5% of GTV, implying roughly a 50% increase from current levels relative to GTV.

Instacart has given little information about the contribution of restaurants to its business. Restaurants are likely to boost growth while weighing on margins, though. Given Instacart's lack of scale, I question whether its restaurants business is actually profitable at the moment. The company has stated that grocery and restaurants reinforce each other, though, meaning restaurants are likely still viewed as a positive overall.

Instacart expects $9-$9.15 billion GTV in Q1, an increase of 8-10% YoY. While this guidance is somewhat soft, there will be roughly a 1% headwind due to 2024 being a leap year. Given current macro uncertainty, Q2 guidance will be critical. While it may still be too early for buyer behavior to be impacted, I believe that there is downside risk to Instacart's growth.

Instacart generated $252 million in adjusted EBITDA and $153 million of operating cash flow in Q4. Cash flows were depressed by working capital fluctuations in the quarter. Instacart's cash flows are generally improving, though, and the company's operating margins have rebounded in recent quarters.

Instacart expects $220-$230 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q1 at roughly a 25% margin. While I expect Instacart's margins to continue improving with scale, particularly if Instacart has greater success in areas like advertising and enterprise solutions, increased competition could be a headwind in time. Smaller average order sizes could also weigh on margins.

Conclusion

Investor sentiment toward many gig economy companies has improved over the past few years as these companies have transitioned to profitability, or at least begun to generate positive free cash flow. I question whether optimism towards some of these companies is overdone in the short term, though.

Instacart's revenue multiple is modest, and the company ended 2024 with liquidity of $1.5 billion. Instacart is also expected to have a mid-teens earnings multiple within the next few years, despite expectations of long-term double-digit growth.

Grocery delivery is an underpenetrated market that currently has reasonable economics and offers opportunities in a number of adjacent markets. As these markets converge, competition is likely to increase, though, and the basis of competition could shift. In particular, I believe there is a risk that the automation of fulfillment will increase the bargaining power of grocers and that success will then be determined by the company with the largest and most efficient delivery network.

As a result, I find it hard to get excited about Instacart's stock at current prices. Consideration also needs to be given to macro weakness and how this would impact Instacart's business. I believe grocery delivery is a luxury that would be hard hit in a recession, exposing investors to significant downside risk.

data.title: RWJ: Value-Heavy But With Low Quality, Worth Avoiding
data.description:
Today's note is supposed to offer a fresh look at the Invesco S&P SmallCap 600 Revenue ETF (NYSEARCA:RWJ), a passively managed vehicle offering exposure to a reweighted version of the S&P 600, with companies with the largest revenues occupying the top spots and vice versa. This is a rather intriguing proposition, but does RWJ live up to expectations?

I have been covering RWJ since 2021, with three notes presented to date and the most recent one published in May 2024. It is true that I have always struggled to find a compelling reason why RWJ should qualify for a Buy rating, so all these articles concluded that it was a Hold.

The gist is that there were a few concerns, chief among them being its critically small exposure to the quality factor that the revenue-centered weighting schema failed to bolster. And today, there will be no upgrade either.

There are two reasons for that.

  • First, RWJ's recent performance amid the tariff saga, despite having a sizable exposure to the value factor, has been merely awful. Sure, it has somewhat stabilized together with the broad market recently, yet its YTD return remains horrible.
  • Second, quality is still the top concern. And in the note, I would like to illustrate that its revenue-centered weighting has not alleviated but deepened some of the S&P 600's quality issues.

So, in sum, I believe that at this juncture, investors should favor high quality (with no compromises) and value, better combined with the size factor (large caps favored over their smaller counterparts), and, alas, RWJ does not fit in that cohort. A Hold rating is thus maintained, with the top reason why I abstained from a bearish thesis being persistent macro uncertainty.

What is The Basis For RWJ's Strategy?

In short, RWJ's strategy is about the S&P 600 but with a twist. As we know from the ETF's website, the basis for its strategy is the S&P SmallCap 600® Revenue-Weighted Index, which

... is constructed using a rules-based approach that re-weights securities of the S&P SmallCap 600® Index according to the revenue earned by the companies, with a maximum 5% per company weighting. The Fund and Index are rebalanced quarterly.

RWJ Performance

What could be the rationale for applying a revenue-centered weighting schema to the S&P 600? In my view, it can do a few things:

  • Build a portfolio that is "more connected to the economic reality", as I called it in the previous notes.
  • Improve valuation characteristics.
  • Potentially even push the weighted-average beta coefficient of the basket lower.
  • And then improve risk dispersion overall, thus allowing the portfolio to become more resilient and less sensitive to stress.

'Less sensitive to stress' means that its drawdowns should not be that steep compared to the S&P 600 ETF and, ideally, to the market. But this has not been the case this year. The recent rebound aside, it has been a mere ordeal, as RWJ has underperformed both the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) and the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), and substantially.

And since my previous skeptical article, RWJ has also significantly underperformed the S&P 500 index.

Sure, its longer-term returns look brighter than the most recent (if we are looking at the full calendar months only, that obviously does not include April yet).

Nevertheless, even here there are a few concerning issues.

  • Since the fund's reorganization in 2019 (the period covered by the table above is June 2019-March 2025), it has delivered a rather solid annualized return, almost beating IVV. However, as I have already explained in the previous article, it was the effect of its meteoric rise in 2021 (52.84% total return vs. IVV's 28.76% and IJR's 26.6%). And over the January 2022-March 2025 period, its annualized return was 0.88%, while IVV delivered 6.65% and IJR's return was -1.27%.
  • This leads us to an important conclusion: RWJ is highly volatile. Its standard deviation of 29.21% illustrates that nicely.
  • Additionally, its maximum drawdown of 37.95% is also telling.

RWJ Portfolio: Revenue Weighting Boosts Value, Quality Remains A Vulnerability

As of April 14, there were 601 equities in RWJ's basket, with consumer discretionary being the key sector with 24.3% weight.

And as we can see above, since IJR's and RWJ's weighting methods are entirely different, despite them having almost the same holdings (there are minor differences when it comes to cash and derivatives, etc.), they have notably different sector proportions.

For example, RWJ is way lighter in financials, IJR's top sector, while substantially heavier in consumer discretionary. But this is just the tip of the iceberg, as things become more interesting when we delve deeper into the factor intricacies.

Let us start with the size and value factors.

Metric RWJ (holdings as of April 14, 2025)* RWJ (holdings as of May 1, 2024) IJR (holdings as of April 14, 2025)
Market Cap $2.45 billion $2.53 billion $3.09 billion
Adjusted EY 6.92% 6.20% 5.69%
P/S 1.05 1.07 3.73
Quant Valuation B- or higher 57.78% 53.80% 34.72%
Quant Valuation D+ or lower 18.38% 18.70% 39.82%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs. *Financial data as of April 15

RWJ has a substantial edge over its peer, as all the metrics presented illustrate. Sure, revenue weighting did a solid job here.

However, as we have already discussed, its stronger weighted-average adjusted earnings yield and other advantages have done little to shore up its performance this year.

Moving to the next factor, this is where RWJ has something of a perennial issue, as, surprisingly, its unconventional weighting method did not contribute to quality characteristics but, contrarily, detracted from them.

Metric RWJ (holdings as of April 14, 2025) RWJ (holdings as of May 1, 2024) IJR (holdings as of April 14, 2025)
ROA 4.13% 3.01% 4.13%
Adjusted ROE 10.37% 11.90% 12.20%
Quant Profitability B- or higher 35.47% 38% 51.32%
Quant Profitability D+ or lower 22.80% 19.20% 12.34%
FCF negative companies 19.32% 19.43% 14.30%
Loss-making companies 23.32% 21.80% 13.84%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

For example, we see that even adjusted for negative and triple-digit figures, its weighted-average Return on Equity is just at 10.37%, below that of IJR. As a reminder, I prefer to see that figure close to 20%, and IVV mostly delivers on that.

Next, the share of companies with serious red flags on profitability (a D+ Quant Profitability grade or weaker) has risen to 22.8%.

Besides, we see that RWJ is much heavier in loss-making and FCF-negative companies than IJR. Just to give a bit more color on this, an example worth mentioning is The Chemours Company (CC), which has negative trailing twelve-months net operating and free cash flows. CC has TTM revenue of $5.78 billion, which, as per my calculations, is 4.9x larger than the median revenue of the S&P 600's constituents. However, as the company is trading at a notably depressed valuation with a diminutive Price/Sales ratio of 0.3x, it has only 15 bps weight in IJR. At the same time, as revenue is what matters for RWJ, its weight is 2.6x larger at 39 bps.

So RWJ's weighting methodology does not immunize it from low quality. On the contrary, at times it amplifies issues.

The growth factor is also worth touching upon briefly.

Metric RWJ (holdings as of April 14, 2025) RWJ (holdings as of May 1, 2024) IJR (holdings as of April 14, 2025)
EPS Fwd 6.50% 4.10% 11.70%
Revenue Fwd 2.86% 3.30% 7.28%
Holdings With Negative Revenue Fwd 32.20% 33.30% 17.07%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Since my previous analysis, the WA forward EPS growth rate has improved while the forward revenue growth rate has declined. Nevertheless, RWJ's basket is still light in growthier companies, and IJR is far ahead of it thanks to weighting (as, for instance, firms with revenues forecast to decline going forward account for a much smaller part of its portfolio). And this makes me conclude that it was RWJ's poor quality that has mostly driven its losses this year, not the growth premia being trimmed.

Final Thoughts

So, is RWJ a good choice in the current market environment? A seemingly pertinent argument is that it has decent valuation characteristics, and value is the right place to be. However, its recent performance has been hideous, despite it having rather comfortable multiples. And I believe its soft quality is to blame. So, my conclusion today is that it is worth avoiding this smart-beta vehicle.

data.title: Estee Lauder Faces Challenges, And It Can Get Worse
data.description:
The Estée Lauder Companies Inc. (NYSE:EL) is a giant in the beauty industry and owns renowned brands, but it is still going through a turbulent period. In part due to its above-average historical profitability rating within its sector (B from Seeking Alpha quant, though it was higher when I began to follow it), I've been tracking the stock since early 2024. It came close to making it through the door into my portfolio, but I'm glad I kept it on the outer aisle of the store, so to speak. Because some stocks can attract buying interest amid a series of negative corporate events. But not this one. This one "stinks" as an investment. At least it has since peaking at $370 to close 2022.

But then, as if it were aiming to do its impersonation of a past-its-prime mall store that sells video games, or a movie theater chain in disrepair, EL did what my late father used to say when his sports team blew a big lead (the NY Knicks, usually): "EL foldo." Yes, EL stock has folded in half multiple times since then. Down 85% in just 15 months. What happened, and will it be a "big dipper" to buy at some point?

You can't put lipstick on this pig of a stock

I don't usually start with technical charts, but for a once-prominent stock and industry leader, there have been no breaks, no bounces that last long enough to enjoy. And, no imminent signs of bottoming.

The longer-term chart (monthly prices) has a usual feature which sums up EL in my second language, technicals: I don't see a PPO (lower indicator) at -30 very often. That's not undervalued, it is underground.

But stock price charts are not everything, even if they are the main thing to me. The fundamental chart below shows a few of the reasons the stock has given up the vast majority of its market capitalization in such a short time. Some reflections of this are revenue declining, cash generation decreasing, and debt rising.

That will cause a stock to give back 13 years or market cap gains in about 13 months.

EL Faces Structural Challenges

There are several reasons for this. In the last quarter, the company's revenue fell by 11% in the Asia/Pacific region, while in the other regions, the decrease was closer to the low single digit. In the breakdown by category, skincare, which is the core of the Estée Lauder Companies, fell by 12%, while make-up was stable.

And this doesn't seem to be coming to an end. Recently, expectations of a continuation of this pressure on sales in Asia were mentioned, and as a consequence, the company plans to make more job cuts.

While these challenges may seem short-term, and in part they are, the company also faces structural challenges. For example, the company is still dependent on the Asian market, and as long as there is uncertainty in this market, as well as global macroeconomic headwinds, it is possible that the fundamentals will continue to slip.

Another crucial factor is competition, not only from other traditional giant players, but also from new, less conventional methods, such as influencer brands, which are gaining ground and consequently taking market share away from more traditional brands.

As a response to these problems, the company has initiated the "Beauty Reimagined" strategic plan, which aims to maintain its focus on the consumer, accelerate innovation, increase investments, and of course, seek the consequence of increasing growth and margins. Although it's good to see that management is seeing the challenges and trying to remain active, the results of these efforts are still quite uncertain, especially given the dynamism of the current market.

EL Valuation Isn’t That Attractive

The highest EPS EL achieved was in fiscal year 2021, reaching a figure of $7.79. This figure would give us a price-to-earnings of 7x, a very attractive level for this industry. But still, that would be the company at full potential, far from what we're seeing now.

As the market constantly revises the outlook according to the current scenario, the expectation now is for a forward price-to-earnings of 22x in view of the fiscal year 2026 EPS, and even in 2028, this multiple only drops to something close to 14.5x.

The valuation may seem very attractive, but it's really only for those who have enough confidence in the company's turnaround. That's not the case for me.

Now, EL is a behemoth in this industry and has the capacity to carry out these optimizations. But this market is changing a lot, and being exposed to these risks and high uncertainty is not interesting to me in the long term.

Factor grades manage to summarize part of this story, indicating only poor valuation, but horrible growth.

Why and how I could end up owning EL anyway

If you are scratching your head about that sub-header, based on every single disparaging word you just read, allow me to explain. I actually have 2 scenarios in which EL could land in my personal portfolio.

1. "Dog Collar:" A quick re-introduction to this approach. Option collars are not new, but my take on it has a bit of a wrinkle in it (pun very much intended!). This involves taking a stock that is badly sold off (like this one is), buying it, but protecting the position by buying puts and selling calls around it. I'm not doing this right now with EL, but here's an example of what it might look like, based on data as of Tuesday's market close:

This is one of many examples, and the pricing on this one is not great, but I'm not buying it now anyway. It also only goes out to 9/19/25, which is short for a stock whose recovery will not be.

So for demonstration purposes, EL is a $54 stock, and I can spend $5.44 a share (per 100 shares I own) to get a combination of puts struck at $55 and calls struck at $70. Bottom line: I can sell the stock at a net price of around $50 a share during this period, and if it rallies to $70, I could be forced to sell it. About 19% upside and 9% downside risk from current levels. If an investor had done this a year ago, they could have limited their loss to as little as 5-10% (it is all customizable), and skated from the 60% decline in the actual stock price.

2. Long-term contrarian stock basket: this is a work in progress at the investing group I run here, but my mission is to build for myself and show subscribers a group of perhaps 20-30 stocks I think can potentially double within 3 years' time. The market's latest move downward simply accelerated my process of filling in the list. But I'm also not convinced I won't get many of them lower. The positions I will take will be very small, and potentially layered in, bought in multiple tranches for each stock. And with a stock basket of that size, the idea is to expect some to continue to implode, but have a small number of them take off over that period. Fundamental strength and/or deep discounted valuation is a part of the equation. Will EL be part of that? I don't know yet. But is it on the list? Definitely.

EL loco?

Here's why: because in the stock market, I never say never. Which is why I'm surprised how often I hear it in the comments section here. I just don't think that way. The merger, new management, a change of fortune few expected, or some other event can turn stocks on a dime. The nature of today's market, much to my chagrin, is that sometimes, all it takes is some buying interest from a motivated, deep-pocket contrarian to turn the tide. No, I don't expect to ever consider EL a "meme stock." But will it be a flyer to consider? Never say never.

data.title: Dream Finders Homes: Good Execution But Terrible Macroeconomic Situation
data.description:
Is it time to buy homebuilders after their recent drawdown? Or is it still too early given the seemingly rapidly deteriorating macroeconomic situation?

Investors have been debating the merits of the sector amid all the news around tariffs, interest rate volatility, Fed policy, and a potential recession later this year.

Dream Finders Homes (NYSE:DFH), for its part, jumps off the page thanks to its mere 6.3x forward P/E ratio along with a sizzling growth rate. Despite that, DFH shares have slumped over the past year and are now back to around where they traded following their IPO in 2021:

While the sell-off has been considerable, I'm not buying DFH stock just yet. Before we get to my reservations, however, let's start with the positives.

The Bull Case For Dream Finders: Outsized Growth At A Cheap Price

Dream Finders has been an absolutely exceptional growth story in recent years. Since 2019, it has grown its annual home closings at a 33% compounded annualized growth rate.

That far exceeds its publicly traded peers. Green Brick Partners (GRBK) grew at about 16% annualized, and most of the others were in the single digits. Similarly, Dream Finders grew earnings per share at a sizzling 54.5% annualized rate since 2019, compared to many homebuilding peers that were in the 20-30% annualized range since that point.

To be clear, this sort of growth is not likely to repeat, either for Dream Finders or the industry as a whole. The pandemic was a once-in-a-generation catalyst for new home buying, as consumers had both elevated demand for new properties and unusually robust financial situations with which to make their purchases.

Regardless, Dream Finders' execution during this period was sensational. And yet, despite that track record, DFM stock continues to trade among the low end of public homebuilding stocks on a P/E basis:

Now, to be fair, you can argue that almost all the homebuilders, aside from NVR (NVR) look really cheap right now. So DFH stock doesn't necessarily stand out when so many names in the industry are in the 6-8x P/E range. But normally, a company with such a tremendous recent growth record would get at least some premium for performance, rather than trading near the bottom of the peer group.

So, Dream Finders is a buy here, right? Not so fast.

Risk Factors Include Geographic Concentration And Valuation Compared To Assets

Purely on an earnings or cash flow basis, Dream Finders looks exceedingly cheap right now. I wouldn't argue that point.

However, homebuilder earnings have a tendency to be highly cyclical. Profitability can erode quickly, or even turn into large losses, during downturns. There were numerous homebuilder bankruptcies in the 2008 cycle, for example, and even the large players with substantial balance sheets saw their share prices get clobbered.

You can't simply slap a P/E ratio on something close to peak earnings and assume that the valuation will hold if the economy goes south.

That's why many investors look at price-to-book ratios when considering homebuilders. The value of a builder's land, inventory, cash, and so on can be a significant buffer that helps support the share price during a downturn in the housing market. Investors should realize that housing is cyclical and just as peak earnings won't last forever, neither will things stay at trough conditions either. So, if book value is considerable, that makes it easier to hold through a downturn.

In the case of Dream Finders, it is still trading at 1.6x book value today. That puts it equal to or above many of its direct peers:

The bullish counterargument here would be that Dream Finders runs more of an asset-light model, trying to not acquire land until it is almost ready to go into construction. This should help ensure that it doesn't get stuck with as much unneeded land and in-progress inventory when the housing cycle turns downward.

That said, there may be limits to how well this insulates Dream Finders in a downturn. As of year-end 2024, the company's controlled lot pipeline was up to 54,698. That was nearly double the count from the company's lot pipeline in 2023. By contrast, home closings in FY'24 were up less than 20% from the prior year, and the company's backlog actually went down year-over-year:

Dream Finders now has $1.7 billion of inventory on its balance sheet, which is just a touch below the firm's current $2.0 billion market cap. In a recession, it's not hard to imagine having to take a meaningful writedown on that inventory, such as through selling new homes at a lower price than previously budgeted. In such a scenario where investors question the value of homebuilders' inventory, we should expect valuation ratios to compress throughout the industry.

Dream Finders' price-to-book value ratio is down to a much more palatable level compared to where it was trading a year ago. But 1.6x book is still fairly steep if industry conditions worsen, and it's not unthinkable Dream Finders could trade to book value in a recession.

I believe falling profit margins could also lead to a lower valuation ratio. The company's profit margins have spiked over the past few years, as the following graph shows:

The company cites four factors that have given it opportunities to improve its profit margins. Among these, we find M&A, faster homebuilding cycles, improved funding and liquidity access, and additional vendor rebates and scale efficiencies.

I'd argue that three of those four are now at risk due to the current macroeconomic backdrop. I expect home sales growth to pause or go into reverse given current macro conditions, which will limit further benefits to scale. And vendors are unlikely to be willing to give out meaningful additional rebates in the current tariff-driven environment. Tariffs and supply chain disruptions are also likely to slow down the speed of new home construction, which ties up capital and reduces margins. This was a major headache for builders during COVID-19, and it could well return now. Finally, persistently higher interest rates along with increasing investor concern about macro conditions mean that Dream Finders is less likely to continue seeing benefits from new funding sources in the near term.

And, on the demand side, Dream Finders lists several risks to its business, such as customer affordability and macroeconomic factors, increased costs associated with lot acquisition, and a rapid increase in financing costs over the past year. On the plus side, the cost of new lots is likely to stop increasing at such a rapid clip given the macroeconomic slowdown.

However, the other two risks loom even larger. Mortgage rates have remained at stubbornly high levels despite the Fed's easing cycle:

Average 30-year fixed mortgages remain close to 7% and are unlikely to get any near-term relief given the recent jitters in the U.S. Treasury bond market. And customers are likely to face more affordability issues in an economic slowdown and potential recession scenario.

My final concern specific to Dream Finders is that it is making a particular geographic bet. Namely, its homebuilding operations are almost exclusively in Sunbelt states across the South and Southwest:

This has been a smart bet over the past few years. These states greatly benefitted from the internal migration patterns during and following the COVID-19 pandemic. The rise of remote work made it easier for people to set up full-time in cheaper cost-of-living states. These were often places with a more attractive taxation and business environment as well. However, this migration pull may start to reverse as more companies return to the office and workers end up moving back to Tier 1 cities outside of the Sunbelt, where the highest-paying jobs tend to be located.

Dream Finders' average home selling price is over $500,000. That's quite a hefty price point, particularly in places where average incomes tend to be lower as compared to states like California, Washington, New York, or Massachusetts. In an economic downturn combined with an employment environment where companies have more leverage to get their workers to return to office, the demand for half-a-million-dollar homes in states like Arizona and South Carolina could take a fairly serious hit.

As a final point there, consider the 2008 housing bust. Much of the hardest-hit areas were in sunbelt states such as Arizona that had minimal restrictions on building and enjoyed a large influx of people from states like California that were seeking cheaper housing. When the cycle turned, however, the bottom dropped out of pricing in several Sunbelt markets as there wasn't sufficient local demand to absorb a glut of newly built units. We are already seeing the possibility for a similar correction this time around, such as the sizable adjustment we're seeing in the Austin, Texas, rental market.

DFH Stock Verdict

I find the macroeconomic outlook for homebuilders to be rather dour at the moment. I'm negative on most of the industry. If interest rates are going to remain persistently high heading into an economic slowdown, that presents a worst-case scenario for housing affordability going forward.

I am at a hold, rather than sell, rating on Dream Finders, however. The company's execution to date has been excellent. And the company's Sunbelt growth strategy combined with the firm's relatively capital-light approach has long-term merit, even if it runs into struggles in the near term. Regardless, it seems things can certainly get worse before they get better for homebuilders and Dream Finders is likely to see more of a cyclical reaction than other larger homebuilding peers given its geographic concentration.

While Dream Finders' 6.3x forward P/E ratio certainly looks cheap, it's risky buying homebuilders based on top-of-cycle earnings multiples. And, on a price-to-book basis, DFH stock has been reaching a more defensible multiple, but 1.6x is far from a floor price in a recession scenario. If the tariff situation is resolved quickly, I could see a case for buying DFH stock as soon as that resolution occurs. But assuming tariffs carry on throughout 2025, I expect a hit to Dream Finders' profit margins combined with falling customer demand. That makes it too early to buy shares today.

data.title: Block: My Top Overall Pick After 40% Plunge
data.description:
Block, Inc. (NYSE:XYZ) has not performed up to par, even when compared against a weakly trading tech sector. It can be hardest to maintain composure when a stock falls so much and so fast, all while having a higher cost basis. At these moments, it is important to reassess the thesis and decide the path forward. While fear is at a peak, I remain of the view that XYZ is a real gem in the tech sector, as its valuation is pricing in devastation. The market is not ready for the strong potential catalysts from Afterpay and the commitment to profitable growth. I see at least triple-digit upside from here, even based on reasonably conservative assumptions.

XYZ Stock Price

I last covered XYZ in January, when I named it one of my top picks for 2025. The stock has been absolutely crushed since then. XYZ has fallen so much that it now trades at the same levels it did in 2017.

Since then, the company has increased gross profits by 10x. The stock was too expensive in 2017, but it is too cheap now.

XYZ Stock Key Metrics

The latest quarter saw the company deliver 14% YoY gross profit growth.

XYZ delivered solid margin expansion, with the adjusted EBITDA margin reaching 33%.

The company also posted a 17% adjusted operating income margin. I remind that this metric does not back out stock-based compensation.

We can see below that operating expenses rose just slightly over the 2023 year. This is not the same company that had a “growth at any cost” reputation during the pandemic. The latest round of layoffs, to the tune of 8% of the workforce, further hammers this point home.

Bears have latched on to the point that Cash App monthly active users have remained stagnant and have shown minimal growth since short-seller Hindenburg raised concerns regarding user tracking in March of 2023.

XYZ has noted that they are focused on “banking the base,” meaning they are focusing on driving growth across profitable clients instead of seeking growth at any cost. I expect user growth to eventually accelerate again, especially given the company will be lapping easier comparables moving forward.

XYZ has seen Square gross payment volume growth accelerate sequentially, driven largely due to international growth. Wall Street appears more focused on the company’s ability to drive further acceleration in U.S. growth moving forward.

Looking ahead, management guided for 2025 to see 15% YoY gross profit growth.

That would have been a highly bullish target given where the stock traded prior to this recent selloff, but the problem was first quarter guidance of just 11%. Wall Street clearly has little confidence that the company can accelerate growth throughout the year.

On the conference call, management noted expectations for the first quarter to be “the lower point throughout the year in terms of gross profit growth and profitability.” Management noted that the first quarter will be negatively impacted by tough banking comps, the leap year (1 pt headwind) and another 1 point headwind from currency fluctuations. Excluding these factors, gross profit perhaps might grow at a 13% to 14% clip in the first quarter, making it more believable for growth to accelerate thereafter on the backs of new products. One of the key catalysts is integrating their buy now pay later Afterpay in the Cash card, which they did in the first quarter. Management outlined expectations for the Cash App to exit 2025 “well above the Q1 gross profit growth rate.” Management also outlined expectations for “Proto, our Bitcoin mining initiative, to start benefiting growth in the second half of the year as well.”

Is XYZ Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

Wall Street does not share the same optimism. XYZ recently traded hands at just 12x this year’s earnings (I note that this is excluding stock-based compensation expenses).

The stock was trading at just around 3.5x gross profits.

I see net margins (based on gross profits) ending up at between 30% to 50%. That places the current valuation at around 10x long-term earnings (based on the low end of 30%), or lower if margins end up higher. With the company still sustaining double-digit top-line growth, the first question that we should ask is if we have mis-assessed the business model. In particular, is XYZ really a bank and is that why its valuation is so low?

Banks typically trade at low earnings multiples due to “deposit risk,” also known as a “run on the bank.” In XYZ’s case, however, that is not the case. We can see below that the company has $8.1 billion of cash, which comfortably covers the $7.5 billion in corporate debt and warehouse funding facilities. The company also had $4.2 billion in customer funds, $2.5 billion in customer receivables, and $1.1 billion in loans held for sale that cover the $5.8 billion in customers payable. That last part is important - the company is not a “real bank” in the deposit sense. It is still holding most of its customer deposits in cash and has made most of its investments in the form of short-term loans and BNPL loans. In contrast, typical banks have invested the vast majority of their customer deposits into long duration investments. At this point in time, there is no “bank run” risk at XYZ.

XYZ may face some economic downturn risk, but following the latest round of layoffs, I expect the company to remain cash flow positive even under the worst of conditions. The company’s Square payment processor and Cash App are both facing competitive risks, but they both operate in big markets and I expect them to still see solid growth even while dealing with competition. The key thing here is the mismatch between valuation and growth, as the balance sheet is very strong as just analyzed. I see fair value for this stock as being between 6x to 9x gross profits. Even at the low end of that range, the stock has nearly 100% potential upside.

XYZ Stock Risks

The main risk here is if I have underestimated the disruption risks from competitors. I think the company is beyond its regulatory risks, but it is always possible that the Cash app faces regulatory issues again. It is possible in the near term that the layoffs are due to the company being unable to meet growth estimates, which might hit the stock again, but I wouldn’t be surprised if the stock has already priced in disappointing results.

XYZ Stock Conclusion

XYZ is my current top overall pick in the market today, based on the unusually low valuation against its respectable thesis. This isn’t a slam dunk play, but the valuation is compelling and management’s clear commitment to driving margins higher should offer considerable downside support - even if that hasn’t proven to be the case lately. XYZ's growing profit margins, net cash balance sheet, and undervaluation explain why it is a core holding of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

data.title: Continue To Avoid J.B. Hunt Transport Services
data.description:
It’s been about 27 ½ months since I suggested investors avoid J.B. Hunt Transport Services, Inc. (NASDAQ:JBHT), and in that time, the shares are down about 27.3% against a gain of about 39% for the S&P 500. The company has just announced earnings, and a stock trading at $125 is an obviously less risky investment than that same stock when it’s trading at $176. I’m going to review the name yet again to see if it’s worth buying at current prices. As usual, I’m going to review the financial results, and I’m going to compare the stock to the risk-free alternative.

Each of my articles comes with a thesis statement very near the beginning. There are a few reasons for this. First, and most importantly, it gives me the opportunity to indulge my “singular” sense of humour, and show off my proper spelling. Second, it gives readers the opportunity to get the “gist” of my thinking before they’re exposed to too much of the aforementioned “humour” and correct spelling. You’re welcome. Anyway, I think it would be prudent to continue to avoid J.B. Hunt because the financial performance since I last reviewed the name has deteriorated markedly. Despite this, the shares aren’t particularly cheap in that they are likely to offer a much lower return than the Treasury Note. Finally, those who know me know that I’m not afraid of excessive repetition, and if you don’t know me, you’re about to find out that I’m not afraid to indulge in excessive repetition. I’ve repeated this point very often, but I think it bears repeating here. If a stock isn’t likely to offer a superior return to the risk-free alternative, why would you buy it?

Financial Snapshot

I’d characterise the financial performance over the past two years as “uninspired.” In 2024, revenue and net income were down by about 5.8% and 21.6% respectively. The most recent financial results suggest that the trend continues lower, with the top and bottom lines being down by about .8%, and 7.7% respectively. Additionally, when we expand our view beyond a year-over-year comparison, things look even worse in my view. For example, a strong argument could be made to suggest that the most recent quarter was much weaker than the same period in 2021. Although revenue in the quarter just ended was 11.6% higher than the same period in 2021, operating income and net income are lower by about 14%, and 19.7% respectively. So, when I wrote “great company” about this business previously, I think I was being myopic, and wrongly assuming that the good times would last.

The story of the capital structure is even worse in my view. Since the first quarter of 2021, long-term debt has increased by $278.2 million, or 21.4%. This might partially account for why interest expense has ballooned by about 55% since then.

So, I’m not impressed with the financial performance here, and I would suggest that this investment has some “hair” on it. That written, the payout ratio is fairly low, and I think even a troubled company can be a great investment if you can acquire it for a reasonable price. I’d be willing to buy this company, warts and all, if, to quote an old game show host “the price is right.”

When I wrote “the price is right” above, I meant “a great price relative to the risk-free alternative.” In order for me to buy at current prices, I need to see the shares offering a superior return when compared to the 10-Year Treasury Note. That’s what the next section of this article will focus on. The reason for this comparison is plain enough to me. A stock comes with risk, and if it offers a lower return than a risk-free investment like a Treasury Note, then there’s no point in buying it. I hope this isn’t a controversial claim.

In terms of the mechanics, when I compare a stock to the Treasury Note, I review the rate at which the stock dividend would need to grow for the cash flows from each investment to be equal. Alternatively, if the yield on the stock is higher than the Treasury Note (a rare thing these days), then I work out the rate at which the dividend can be cut to match Treasury cash flows. I then work out whether or not this growth is likely given financial history.

As of this writing, the yield on the 10-Year Treasury Note is about 4.3%, and the dividend yield on JB Hunt is about 1.3%. Not only have I calculated the rate of growth necessary for the cash flows from the dividend to match the cash flows of the Treasury Note, but I present my findings in the table below for your enjoyment and edification. In order for the stock investors to earn the same cash flows as the investors who buy the much less risky Treasury, the dividend will need to grow at a CAGR of about 25% for a decade. I know the payout ratio is currently low at ~30%, but this is extraordinarily high, and a very optimistic assumption, particularly for a cyclical business such as this.

In case you’re wondering, over the past decade, the dividend has grown at a (quite impressive) CAGR of about 8%. That’s impressive in my view, but I’m not sure that that growth rate is sustainable, let alone 25%.

Obviously stocks, just like Treasury Notes, come with capital gain potential. Therefore, it’s worth considering whether or not an investor in this stock can close the gap with the Treasury Note via price appreciation. Obviously, it’s possible, but that’s a much more risky part of the return. Treasury Note holders know the exact size and timing of their cash flows, just like stock investors have a fairly good understanding of when their (hopefully growing) dividends come in. Price movement is much less certain, as demonstrated by the past few months. That portion of the return is less certain, so I discount it. Put another way, someone who bought when I was suggesting people eschew the shares is sitting on a significant capital loss. In order for that person to match the Treasury Note, that loss will need to be made up, and then some.

Given the above, I think it prudent to continue to avoid these shares until the price drops further, and/or the yield jumps higher.

data.title: Low-Volatility Strategy Is 2025's Upside Outlier For Equity Factors
data.description:
Standing alone among US equity factors this year, the low-volatility strategy is holding on to a modest gain year to date. In sharp relief, the rest of the factor field is posting varying degrees of loss, based on a set of ETFs through yesterday’s close (Apr. 15).

The iShares MSCI USA Minimum Volatility Factor ETF (USMV) is up 2.7% year to date. By contrast, the US stock market overall (SPY) is down 8.0%. All the other factor ETFs are also in the red so far this year.

The deepest factor loss in 2025: small-cap value stocks, which have crashed 18.9%, based on iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Value ETF (IJS).

Although most slices of the US equity market have taken a hit this year, small-cap value’s fall from grace has been unusually harsh. Perhaps the reversal of fortunes is related to IJS’s previously high-flying performance.

Consider the perspective of investing equal dollar amounts five years ago in small-cap value (IJS), low vol (USMV) and the broad equity market (SPY). As recently as early December 2024, small-cap value was outperforming both funds over that time frame.

In recent months, however, the tables turned quickly and dramatically this year for IJS, although it’s still outperforming low vol (USMV) over the trailing 5-year window.

All of which reminds that cycles dominate equity factors. With that in mind, the hefty haircut for small-cap value of late may be laying the foundation for another bull run for these stocks. Timing, as always, is uncertain. Meanwhile, value/contrarian-oriented investors may do well by looking for signs of a bottom in IJS, perhaps at some point in the near future.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Forget The Tariffs: 3 Chip Stocks To Buy Now
data.description:
The back and forth with China trade tariffs has become nauseating, especially if you are a tech investor. In the most recent move, NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and other top chipmakers were "informed" by the U.S. government that they would need licenses to sell advanced chips in China.

This news came on the back of the administration just exempting technology companies from the ludicrously high tariffs. So, we are continuing to see the back and forth, contributing to the uncertainty and enabling increased anxiety and volatility here.

The China Tensions Could Cost Nvidia $5.5 Billion

Subsequently, Nvidia put out a statement that the China restrictions could cost it about $5.5 billion. The stock was lower by about 7% after the news. Similarly, Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) may face issues due to the China tariffs, as sales of its MI308 and other products could get hit. Its stock dropped by a comparable margin.

Many chip stocks are dipping again this morning, but the third stock I want to highlight here is Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL). Marvell was also down, by about 3% this morning, with its total peak to trough decline now around 67%.

Despite the recent "tariff meltdown," AI growth remains robust. Therefore, the market may be overly negative on some high-quality chip companies.

Many high-quality companies like the chips stocks mentioned, even with the China tariffs, could remain highly profitable, and in the best-case outcome, we could even get a trade deal done with China.

No One Wins In A Trade War

That's right. No one really wins in a trade war, which is especially painful in the near term. China needs the U.S.'s consumer market, and the U.S. needs China's manufacturing hub. There is no other $3 trillion-plus import market.

So, where will China dump its excess production if not in the USA? This dynamic threatens the Chinese economy greatly, and there is a risk of a very substantial downturn in China if a trade deal is not achieved relatively soon.

Let's Be Real Here

Come on, let's be real here. The U.S. probably should not be manufacturing many products, but it should manufacture some things, especially necessities critical to its national interests and security.

China should not have a global monopoly on manufacturing batteries, rare earths, or any other crucial materials essential to the U.S., its allies, and in particular, essential to the West's defense/military.

China Is An Adversarial Communist Nation

I like China very much, but it's an adversarial nation that actually threatens and challenges the U.S.'s global dominance economically, militarily, and geopolitically.

Moreover, this is a first for the U.S. As in previous instances in history, the U.S. competed against traditional allies like England and Europe economically, while militarily, it dealt with a relatively weak economic USSR.

China, on the other hand, is very different. It is attempting to surpass the U.S. economically in many respects while being its greatest military adversary at the same time. Again, this is a new dynamic for the U.S., and China requires a unique approach.

A Trade Deal Can Get Accomplished

At the end of the day, it may be smart for both nations (the U.S. and China) to reach an acceptable trade agreement. This dynamic should decrease uncertainty and improve sentiment. High-quality chip stocks like Nvidia, AMD, and Marvell are already cheap, and there should be considerable AI growth ahead. Moreover, a settlement with China could send their stocks skyrocketing.

Valuations Have Cratered

I know what you may be thinking. What good are valuations here if profitability declines? Well, my view is that even if profitability declines, it should be transitory, and these companies have excellent growth and profitability prospects, even if problems with China continue.

1. Nvidia - Nvidia trades at a forward P/E ratio of about 20, relatively low and even if the profitability decreases temporarily, it has substantial profitability prospects ahead. Meanwhile, the stock has now become relatively cheap.

Many people wanted to own Nvidia's stock when it was around $150. So, they should be loving the stock around $100 today. While there are increased uncertainties, fundamentally, not much has changed at Nvidia. It remains the AI kingpin and likely has considerable growth ahead.

2. AMD - AMD has gotten down to a ridiculously cheap multiple, now trading at only about 14 times forward EPS estimates. AMD is similar to Nvidia, in the respect that the stock has gotten much cheaper, but not much has changed from a fundamental perspective. AMD may go through a transitory earnings decline phase, but the stock price appears to have priced in a considerable decline already.

3. Marvell - Marvell similarly trades at only about 14 times next year's earnings projections. This stock has been hammered worse than most. After being one of the highest AI hardware flyers, this stock has also dropped more than most. This dynamic suggests that Marvell could have substantial upside ahead as hardware AI chip stocks recover after the uncertainty fades and a more stable environment returns.

data.title: Behind The Year-End Surge In Equity Financing Demand
data.description:
By Vijay Sumon

At a Glance

  • As the S&P 500 hit record highs at year-end, dealers faced a significant increase in equity collateral financing needs
  • Adjusted Interest Rate Total Return futures on U.S. indices are designed to give total return exposure with an overnight floating rate built in

Heading into year-end, equity repo rates can fluctuate significantly due to increased upside demand and reduced balance sheet supply. Higher spreads in the bi-lateral OTC market make equity financing more expensive, pushing market participants to seek cost-effective alternatives.

U.S. Equity Repo Levels Rise in Q4

Balance sheet management for primary dealers plays a crucial role in equity financing, as these institutions can face limitations on the amount of equity funding they can provide. The sharp increase in equity repo funding spreads during November and December 2024 indicates a period of tighter funding conditions due to bank balance sheet constraints.

Amid these conditions, the January contracts were then examined in more detail since they bridge ‌the year-end dates and as a result experienced a surge in trading volume.

Market Participant Positioning

Toward the end of 2024, as the S&P 500 market touched record highs, dealers held record long equity inventory to finance buy-side upside exposure. This was mostly facilitated by dealers, who as a result were long stock inventory in order to replicate short futures positions they had sold.

The CFTC Commitments of Traders reports showed dealers reducing long physical positioning through derivative or other financial instruments, such as long S&P 500 AIR TRFs. This is described by many as year-end balance sheet management.

What was interesting about Q4 2024 was that as demand drove equity financing costs higher, asset managers added equity funding supply to the market, almost doubling net open interest (OI) in the AIR TRF market.

The increase in participation by non-traditional equity repo counterparties, like the buy-side in the Commitments of Traders reports was a contributing factor in driving AIR TRF notional OI to $275B in December 2024, representing an increase 130% year-on-year.

While equity repo trading is one use case for AIR TRFs, growing adoption can also be attributed to the impact of regulatory changes on OTC swap capital efficiency as well as the contract's similar economics to equity index total return swaps (TRS) but with added liquidity and transparency.

As primary dealers face balance sheet constraints, AIR TRFs offer greater access and allows the buyside to participate in what was previously a dealer-to-dealer market.

For various market participants looking to navigate the complexities of the year-end period and manage their equity exposures more effectively, AIR TRF futures are proving to be a valuable tool in their investment strategies.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
